ECO10005-Economics for Business Decision Making
Introduction (Main Concepts)
Professor Abbas Valadkhani
Week 1

ECO10005
This provides an introduction to both microeconomic and macroeconomic theories with many practical applications.
This unit will assist you to be “future business decision-makers” by motivating  you with contemporary economic issues highlighted by the expert views of industry and government representatives.
You will use economic concepts to improve decision-making for businesses and government policy outcomes including: How prices and output are set by firms to maximise profit, and how firms respond to changes in market conditions.

Assessment Summary
•	Two assignments in weeks 5 and 11 (20%)
•	Microeconomics online test  (15%)
•	Macroeconomic online test (15%)
•	Final assessment (50%)

Economics is the study of the choices people and societies make to attain their unlimited wants, given their scarce resources.
Economics: foundations and models (2 of 2)

Scarcity and trade-offs
Scarcity: The situation in which unlimited wants exceed the limited resources available to fulfill those wants.
Resources: Inputs used to produce goods and services, including natural resources such as land, water and minerals, labour, capital, and entrepreneurial ability.
Trade-off: The idea that, because of scarcity, producing more of one good or service means producing less of another good or service.

Economic models
To develop a model economists generally follow these steps:
Decide on the assumptions to be used in developing the model.
Formulate a testable hypothesis.
Use economic data to test the hypothesis.
Revise the model if it fails to explain the economic data.
Retain the revised model to help answer similar economic questions in the future.

Using graphs and formulae
Economic models, like graphs and formulas, can be compared to maps—they provide a simplified guide to reality.
Reproduced with the kind permission of National Roads and Motorists’ Association Limited.

Normative and positive analysis
Positive analysis: Analysis concerned with what is, involving value-free statements that can be checked by using the facts.
Normative analysis: Analysis concerned with what ought to be, involving value judgements which cannot be tested.

Microeconomics and macroeconomics
Microeconomics: The study of how households and firms make choices, how they interact in markets, and how the government attempts to influence their choices.
Macroeconomics: The study of the economy as a whole, including topics such as inflation, unemployment, and economic growth.

Increasing marginal opportunity cost: Figure 2.2

Economic growth
Economic growth: The expansion of society’s production potential.
Economic growth can be illustrated using the production possibility frontier.
Production possibility frontiers and real-world trade-offs

Shifting out the production possibility frontier: Economic growth: Figure 2.3

We use the production possibility frontier and the concept of opportunity cost to explain the economic gains from specialisation and trade.
Trade: The act of buying or selling a good or service in a market.
Comparative advantage and trade

We use a simple example of two people—you and your neighbour.
And two goods, apples and cherries, measured in kilograms (kgs).

Opportunity costs

Absolute advantage: The ability of an individual, firm or country to produce more of a good or service than competitors using the same amount of resources.
Comparative advantage: The ability of an individual, firm, or country to produce a good or service at a lower opportunity cost than other producers.
Absolute advantage versus comparative advantage

Why do you need to study economics?
Let me give you an example

Due to the COVID-19, the government and central banks conducted unprecedented expansionary fiscal and monetary policies.

The law of conservation
In a chemical reaction mass  is neither created nor destroyed.
For example, the carbon atom in coal becomes carbon dioxide when it is burned.
The carbon atom changes from a solid structure to a gas but its mass does not change.

Any expected consequences?
But the central banks first thought inflation would be transitory despite the associated 18-24 months lags and the fact that:

Misread Inflationary Signals
Ceteris paribus, if the RBA resists rate hikes while other central banks increase their benchmark rates, the Australian dollar depreciates.
Depreciation of the Australian dollar leads to more expensive imported goods and services in the CPI basket.
The RBA may need to raise the cash rate further and faster in response to increased import prices.
‹#›

Conflicting signals
The yield curve inversion in the US bond market suggests a hard landing (pending recession).
The US equity market suggest a soft or no landing.
